home.heroLine1: Welcome to a refined world of puzzles where intuition meets logic.
home.cardPairLinkDesc: An intuitive logic puzzle: connect matching characters without crossing lines. Fill every cell and aim for a clean clear.
home.cardReflecDesc: A ball-and-bumper logic puzzle: predict mirror reflections to reach the goal. Builds spatial reasoning.
home.cardSkyDesc: A full logic puzzle: deduce building heights from edge clues. When stuck, let “Maybe Bee” nudge your reasoning.
home.cardPresTitle: Pres-Sure Judge (プレッシャージャッジ)
home.cardPresDesc: The NPC loads weights and the scale tips—stay balanced within 10 seconds. Wrong judgments stack in this survival-style game.
home.columnCta: AI × Montessori: learn how each puzzle supports development
home.sectionColumnBody: A fixed article summarizing each title’s developmental benefits from neuroscience, Montessori practice, and difficulty design—reference material for parents and educators.
home.sectionColumnLink: AI × Montessori: read the full educational column
home.sectionAboutBody: Wispo is a free site of educational logic puzzles that nurture logical thinking and intuition. Enjoy Pair-Link, Skyscraper, Reflec-Shot, Pres-Sure Judge (プレッシャージャッジ), and more in your browser.
home.sectionUsageBeforeContact: All games are free. Rules and controls are on each screen. Contact us via the
home.sectionUsageMid: . For terms and privacy, see the

reflecShot.footerBlurb: Grid-logic prototype. Entry and goal sit on cells adjacent to open bottom/top edges; solve L/T shapes with bumpers (／＼－｜) and wall rules. Higher grades use larger boards.

pairLink.p_dev: Pair-Link (Numberlink) links matching endpoints with non-crossing paths that visit every cell—three coupled constraints in a classic paper puzzle. On paper, erasing is slow and feedback lags. In Wispo, drag-draw and undo are instant so retries do not break the rhythm of thought. Stylus or finger input ties fine motor control to spatial imagery, continuous with school-age drawing tasks.
pairLink.p_neuro: Searching under both no-crossing and full-grid rules repeatedly engages error prediction and strategy updating in the prefrontal cortex. Holding a tentative path in working memory while lookahead checks dead-ends loads visuospatial working memory—akin to map reading and net reasoning. Well-paced repetition supports schema formation, not rote memorization.
pairLink.p_mont: Montessori demonstrations show only minimal order (“no crossings,” “no gaps”); path discovery stays with the child. Rule breaks return immediately as interaction, enabling self-correction without adult scolding—consistent with auto-education in a prepared environment.
pairLink.p_step: Grades scale board size, pair count, and generator constraints. Early boards keep the search space small for achievable wins; later grades demand longer hypothesis maintenance and planning. Viewing lines as graph edges seeds discrete-math intuition. When adults scaffold, naming “where it gets risky next” supports metacognition more than giving answers.

skyscraper.p_dev: Skyscraper combines Latin-square uniqueness with edge “visible skyscraper” counts. Paper erasure is tedious; digital play makes trial verification cheap so attention stays on constraint reasoning. One rule set supports children through adults by varying depth, not mechanics.
skyscraper.p_neuro: Each contradiction triggers prediction-error processing and frontal update loops. Simulating occlusion from a bird’s-eye view couples spatial inference with magnitude comparison. “Maybe” placements externalize working memory, scaffolding the distinction between hypotheses and committed digits—useful persistence for case analysis in math and science.
skyscraper.p_mont: Order, precision, and concentration map to a calm board that reflects each action—external order supporting inner order. Maybe-mode is support, not substitution: the child remains the decision-maker, matching self-education principles.
skyscraper.p_step: Board size and presets widen the search space gradually. Small boards make strong clue patterns tangible; larger boards build tolerance for compound constraints—parallel to sustained case-splitting stamina in arithmetic.

reflecLab.p_dev: Reflec-Shot abstracts mirrors and walls into a grid with bumper symbols (／＼－｜), letting learners decide trajectories logically. Physical kits cost setup and safety; angles vary. Digital motion applies reflection rules exactly, turning hypotheses into visible outcomes in tight feedback loops.
reflecLab.p_neuro: Clear success/failure per trial rehearses outcome monitoring. Predicting cell-wise motion updates direction sequences, loading working memory like maze tasks. Long-press then swipe to commit a bumper type links motor planning with rapid strategy choice—near cognitive flexibility training. Explicit goal rules ease error attribution.
reflecLab.p_mont: Consistent environmental feedback matches materials that make errors obvious without lecturing. Children map failures to their own action sequences, reducing adult micromanagement. Demonstrations can show only the minimal order of reflection; exploration remains the child’s work.
reflecLab.p_step: Higher grades add cells and bumper complexity, moving from single-bounce prediction to multi-bounce chains. Early clears support normalized ego strength (the feeling of completing work independently); harder stages train sustained attention and planning horizons. The lab build invites small-scale observation before wider classroom use.

column.title: Educational column: digital logic puzzles and children’s thinking
column.introHeading: Introduction: interactive environments and Montessori auto-education
column.introP1: Wispo connects what digital media afford—immediate feedback, staged challenges, and low-cost trial-and-error—with Montessori ideas of auto-education and the prepared environment inside one play experience.
column.introP2: Software does not grade the child; it behaves as a transparent environment with consistent rules. Algorithmic generation echoes how adults sequence materials and tasks for developmental stages—without chasing buzzwords. It stands on durable ideas: trial, error, and correction; order and concentration; autonomy grounded in real mastery.
column.introP3: Below we survey Pair-Link, Reflec-Shot, Skyscraper, Pres-Sure Judge, and related content across design intent, cognitive neuroscience, Montessori practice, and difficulty curves—as talking points for parents and teachers.
column.tocPres: Pres-Sure Judge (プレッシャージャッジ)
column.s1pDev: Linking equal endpoints with a single path, covering all cells, and forbidding crossings is a long-standing pencil puzzle. Digitizing minimizes the cost of drawing, undoing, and retrying so thinking keeps its pace. Stylus and touch tie fine motor acts to spatial representations, linking to school-age graphic work.
column.s1pNeuro: Dual constraints (no crossings, full grid) repeatedly cue error prediction and strategy updating in the prefrontal cortex. Holding a provisional route while lookahead checks dead-ends loads spatial working memory, overlapping map-reading and unfolding-space reasoning. Thoughtful repetition encourages schema selection.
column.s1pMont: Brief demos show only the order (no crossings, no gaps) and leave route discovery to the child—bridging toward auto-education without hand-holding. Rule violations return instantly as interaction, offering correction independent of external judgment.
column.s1pStep: Grades (G1+) scale board size, pair count, and generator constraints. Early wins stabilize motivation; later grades widen the search space. Viewing lines as graph edges foreshadow discrete-math intuition.
column.s2pDev: Mirrors fascinate hands-on, yet lab setups are costly and noisy. Abstracting to a grid with wall and bumper symbols reproduces uniform motion and reflection digitally, safely cycling geometric intuition and symbolic reasoning.
column.s2pNeuro: Clear trajectories rehearse performance monitoring. Remembering turn sequences taxes working memory; committing a bumper after long-press + swipe couples motor planning with rapid decisions—near cognitive flexibility training.
column.s2pMont: Failure modes (facing a wall, returning to start, missing the goal) are explicit on-screen, echoing materials that make errors visible from the environment. Children align rules with their own moves, enabling self-evaluation with less adult interruption.
column.s2pStep: Grades grow board size and bumper complexity, moving from single-bounce prediction to multi-bounce inference. Early success supports normalized development of ego strength; later stages demand sustained attention and planning. The public lab suits small-scale observation before wider rollout.
column.s3pDev: Placing 1…N once per row and column while edge tallies match visible towers fuses Latin-square structure with visibility counting. Paper erasure is heavy; digital play lightens trials so effort stays on constraint reasoning.
column.s3pNeuro: Contradictory placements drive repeated prediction-error processing. Mental simulation of occlusion from above binds spatial inference to magnitude comparison. Maybe-mode externalizes working memory, scaffolding hypothesis versus commitment.
column.s3pMont: Order, precision, and concentration align with a quiet board that mirrors each action—external order nurturing inner order. Maybe-mode remains support; the child stays the agent.
column.s3pStep: Board size and presets widen the hypothesis space stepwise. Small boards make salient patterns tangible; large boards build stamina against compound constraints—parallel to sustained case analysis in school math.
column.s4Title: 4. Pres-Sure Judge (プレッシャージャッジ)
column.s4pDev: After the NPC places weights, players drag from inventory to pans under time pressure—hard to stage on paper with synchronized tilt and countdown. Digital coupling makes mistakes immediate; survival-style accumulation across rounds stresses strategy and self-regulation more than single-shot correctness.
column.s4pNeuro: Short windows for pan choice, weight choice, and Judge timing engage executive function—especially inhibitory control and rapid state updating—in the prefrontal cortex. Tracking the moving scale with the finger recruits visuomotor integration; holding numeric torque imagery doubles working-memory load. Frequent NPC moves drive prediction-error loops; longer sessions add arousal and sustained attention—training-like endurance for school-age executive skills.
column.s4pMont: Scale tilt offers concrete causality before abstract lecture—close to sensorial materials. Immediate feedback on misplacement or timeout supports correction from the environment, not scolding. The NPC models another agent’s move—a thin social frame for “how do I respond after someone else acts?” Reducing adult chatter while outcomes return through the environment aligns with auto-education.
column.s4pStep: Rounds shift NPC pressure and inventory tension, moving from single-weight comparison to multi-step balance imagery. The Judge action marks decision boundaries, separating impulse from inference. Gradual difficulty preserves a prepared gradient of load, integrating number, time, and movement beyond pure logic puzzles.
